verses.text: Now king David was old and stricken in years; and they covered him with clothes, but he gat no heat. Wherefore his servants said unto him, Let there be sought for my lord the king a young virgin: and let her stand before the king, and let her cherish him, and let her lie in thy bosom, that my lord the king may get heat. So they sought for a fair damsel throughout all the coasts of Israel, and found Abishag a Shunammite, and brought her to the king. And the damsel was very fair, and cherished the king, and ministered to him: but the king knew her not. Guidance: 1 Kings opens with David's old age. In the Hebrew Bible 1-2 Kings is one continuous book with 1-2 Samuel — together called the Books of Kingdoms; the chapter break between Samuel and Kings is artificial. The narrative simply continues from the threshing-floor altar of 2 Samuel 24 to the king's final days. The opening detail is poignant. The man who fought Goliath at fifteen, who was strong enough that 'a bow of steel was broken by mine arms' (2 Sam 22:35), is now so frail he cannot stay warm under his blankets. The Hebrew lo-yicham — 'he could not be warmed' — describes circulatory failure, age-related body-cold. His servants suggest a remedy that strikes modern readers as strange: a young woman to share his bed for warmth. The cultural context is medical — a young person's body heat was thought to help the elderly recover. They search the country and find Abishag the Shunammite — Shunem being a town in the territory of Issachar (later associated with the great Shunammite woman of 2 Kings 4). Two facts about her: she was 'very fair' (yaphah me'od — very beautiful); and 'the king knew her not.' The narrator is careful to specify that the relationship remained non-sexual. The detail will matter when Adonijah, in chapter 2, asks for Abishag as his wife — which Solomon will read as a back-door claim to the throne (since taking a king's woman was an act of royal usurpation). The chapter is laying the ground for the succession crisis: the king is too weak to govern; the kingdom is functionally unattended; ambitious sons will move.

Then Adonijah the son of Haggith exalted himself, saying, I will be king: and he prepared him chariots and horsemen, and fifty men to run before him. And his father had not displeased him at any time in saying, Why hast thou done so? and he also was a very goodly man; and his mother bare him after Absalom. Guidance: Adonijah moves. He is David's fourth son — born after Absalom, who is dead; Chileab/Daniel, who probably died young; and Amnon, killed by Absalom. With these three older brothers dead, Adonijah considers himself heir-presumptive by primogeniture. But David had earlier promised the throne to Solomon (the chapter will refer to this oath in v17, 30). Adonijah, knowing this, decides to preempt the LORD's choice by self-coronation. 'Exalted himself' (mitnasse) — the same verb used for Absalom's earlier rebellion. The chapter is signaling that the same pattern is recurring. 'I will be king' — the bald self-declaration. He prepares the regalia of kingship: chariots, horsemen, fifty footmen running before him — exactly the procession Absalom had organized in 2 Sam 15:1. The narrator's parenthesis is devastating: 'His father had not displeased him at any time in saying, Why hast thou done so?' David, for all his greatness, was a poor disciplinarian of his sons. He never said 'no' to Adonijah. The pattern is consistent across David's failures as a father — Amnon was not punished for raping Tamar; Absalom was not punished for murdering Amnon; Adonijah is not corrected. The chapter is locating Adonijah's coup in family-systems terms: it is the predictable fruit of permissive fatherhood. And 'he also was a very goodly man' (tov-to'ar me'od) — handsome, charismatic. The Bible is honest that physical attractiveness combined with parental indulgence produces dangerous adults. The chapter is teaching us a sobering lesson about the formation of royal character: looks plus permissiveness equals usurpation.

And he conferred with Joab the son of Zeruiah, and with Abiathar the priest: and they following Adonijah helped him. But Zadok the priest, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and Nathan the prophet, and Shimei, and Rei, and the mighty men which belonged to David, were not with Adonijah. Guidance: The court splits. Adonijah's backers: Joab (David's commander, who had repeatedly outraged the king by murdering Abner, Amasa, and Absalom against David's express wish — Joab knew his career would end the moment Solomon ascended) and Abiathar the priest (descendant of Eli, David's loyal companion through the wilderness years, but apparently nervous about Solomon's accession). Solomon's backers: Zadok the priest (a separate priestly line), Benaiah son of Jehoiada (commander of David's bodyguard — the lion-slayer from 2 Sam 23:20), Nathan the prophet (the man who confronted David about Bathsheba and whose name appears in Solomon's birth-name 'Jedidiah'), Shimei and Rei (otherwise unknown — perhaps Shimei son of Elah, later one of Solomon's twelve officers in 1 Kings 4:18), and 'the mighty men' (the Gibborim — David's personal bodyguard, including the Thirty-Seven of 2 Sam 23). The chapter is showing us that the kingdom is not split fifty-fifty. Adonijah has two senior men with him; Solomon has the prophet, the bodyguard commander, the alternative priest, and the elite warriors. Numerically the contest looks even; institutionally, Solomon's party holds the muscle. The chapter is preparing us to understand why Adonijah's coup will collapse so quickly when David finally responds.

And Adonijah slew sheep and oxen and fat cattle by the stone of Zoheleth, which is by Enrogel, and called all his brethren the king's sons, and all the men of Judah the king's servants: But Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah, and the mighty men, and Solomon his brother, he called not. Guidance: The coronation banquet. Adonijah throws a sacrificial feast at 'the stone of Zoheleth' (the Hebrew may mean 'serpent stone' — possibly a known landmark) by En-rogel, a spring just southeast of Jerusalem. Sacrificing oxen, sheep, and fattened animals on this scale signals a formal political event — and quite possibly a coronation ritual. He invites all of David's other sons (his eligible siblings) and the men of Judah serving at court. But the guest list reveals the truth: 'Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah, and the mighty men, and Solomon his brother, he called not.' The omission of Solomon is the giveaway. A general inclusive feast for all the king's sons would have included Solomon as a matter of course; pointedly excluding him reveals that the feast is in fact a counter-coronation. Nathan and Benaiah are excluded because they would oppose; the mighty men are excluded because their loyalty is to David and the LORD's chosen successor. The chapter is showing us how political pretense works: a religious feast in the country, ostensibly innocent, in fact the launch of a usurpation. Nathan, in the next paragraph, sees through it instantly.

Wherefore Nathan spake unto Bathsheba the mother of Solomon, saying, Hast thou not heard that Adonijah the son of Haggith doth reign, and David our lord knoweth it not? Now therefore come, let me, I pray thee, give thee counsel, that thou mayest save thine own life, and the life of thy son Solomon. Go and get thee in unto king David, and say unto him, Didst not thou, my lord, O king, swear unto thine handmaid, saying, Assuredly Solomon thy son shall reign after me, and he shall sit upon my throne? why then doth Adonijah reign? Behold, while thou yet talkest there with the king, I will also come in after thee, and confirm thy words. Guidance: Nathan organizes the counter-move. He speaks to Bathsheba — Solomon's mother — not directly to David. This is shrewd diplomacy. Bathsheba, as the king's wife and the favored son's mother, has standing to enter the king's bedchamber alone; Nathan does not. Together their joint testimony will be decisive. Nathan's message is direct. (1) He frames the crisis: 'Adonijah the son of Haggith doth reign, and David our lord knoweth it not.' (Calling Adonijah 'son of Haggith' rather than 'son of David' subtly diminishes his claim — locating his royalty in his mother's name rather than David's.) (2) He underlines the stakes: 'save thine own life, and the life of thy son Solomon.' Nathan is realistic — in ancient succession crises, the losing prince and his mother were usually killed. (3) He gives Bathsheba her line: remind David of his oath that Solomon would reign. (4) He arranges the two-witness pattern: Nathan will follow Bathsheba into the chamber and corroborate her words. Two independent witnesses in immediate succession will be impossible to dismiss as a setup. The chapter is showing us a master political tactician at work. Nathan, the prophet who once confronted David alone over Bathsheba, here uses Bathsheba to confront David, and his timing is exquisite — Solomon's mother has the maternal voice; he has the prophetic voice; together they form a chord David cannot ignore.

And Bathsheba went in unto the king into the chamber: and the king was very old; and Abishag the Shunammite ministered unto the king. And Bathsheba bowed, and did obeisance unto the king. And the king said, What wouldest thou? And she said unto him, My lord, thou swarest by the LORD thy God unto thine handmaid, saying, Assuredly Solomon thy son shall reign after me, and he shall sit upon my throne. And now, behold, Adonijah reigneth; and now, my lord the king, thou knowest it not: And he hath slain oxen and fat cattle and sheep in abundance, and hath called all the sons of the king, and Abiathar the priest, and Joab the captain of the host: but Solomon thy servant hath he not called. And thou, my lord, O king, the eyes of all Israel are upon thee, that thou shouldest tell them who shall sit on the throne of my lord the king after him. Otherwise it shall come to pass, when my lord the king shall sleep with his fathers, that I and my son Solomon shall be counted offenders. Guidance: Bathsheba enters David's chamber. The detail is poignant: 'the king was very old; and Abishag the Shunammite ministered unto the king.' Two of David's women are now in the same room — the long-time wife Bathsheba and the recent attendant Abishag. The narrator is sketching the visual: the dying king, the attending young woman, the wife with grave news. Bathsheba bows. The king asks 'What wouldest thou?' — a routine formula but here loaded; the kingdom hangs on the next sentence. She delivers Nathan's message exactly as instructed. (1) Reminds David of his oath: 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me.' (2) Reports Adonijah's coup: 'now Adonijah reigneth.' (3) Names the participants: Abiathar, Joab, all the king's other sons — and the absence: 'Solomon thy servant hath he not called.' (4) Appeals to David's responsibility: 'the eyes of all Israel are upon thee, that thou shouldest tell them who shall sit on the throne.' (5) States the personal stakes: 'I and my son Solomon shall be counted offenders' (chatta'im — sinners, the political category for those executed by a new king for being on the wrong side). Bathsheba is brief, factual, and decisive. The woman who in 2 Sam 11 was a passive figure — bathed, summoned, used, widowed — is here a political agent securing her son's throne. The chapter is showing us her growth. The traumas of her early life have not destroyed her; she has become a queen-mother capable of decisive action.

And, lo, while she yet talked with the king, Nathan the prophet also came in. And they told the king, saying, Behold Nathan the prophet. And when he was come in before the king, he bowed himself before the king with his face to the ground. And Nathan said, My lord, O king, hast thou said, Adonijah shall reign after me, and he shall sit upon my throne? For he is gone down this day, and hath slain oxen and fat cattle and sheep in abundance, and hath called all the king's sons, and the captains of the host, and Abiathar the priest; and, behold, they eat and drink before him, and say, God save king Adonijah. But me, even me thy servant, and Zadok the priest, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and thy servant Solomon, hath he not called. Is this thing done by my lord the king, and thou hast not shewed it unto thy servant, who should sit on the throne of my lord the king after him? Guidance: Nathan enters on cue — Bathsheba is still speaking when he arrives. The two witnesses have established proximity-in-time; the king cannot dismiss either testimony as isolated. Nathan's question is brilliant rhetoric: 'Hast thou said, Adonijah shall reign after me?' He does not accuse David of changing his mind; he asks whether David, perhaps in old-age forgetfulness, has reversed his oath. The question forces David to deny that he authorized Adonijah's coup. Nathan then reports the same scene Bathsheba had reported, with one new vivid detail: 'they eat and drink before him, and say, God save king Adonijah.' (Hebrew yechi ha-melek Adoniyahu — Long live King Adonijah; the coronation acclamation.) The coup is no longer mere preparation; it has reached the formal acclamation stage. Nathan closes with the personal twist: 'me, even me thy servant, and Zadok the priest, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and thy servant Solomon, hath he not called.' The chapter is preserving the four-name list of the loyalist faction. Then the question that pierces David: 'Is this thing done by my lord the king, and thou hast not shewed it unto thy servant?' Nathan, the king's most trusted prophet, asks whether the king has acted without informing him. The implicit appeal is to the long relationship of trust between David and Nathan. David cannot let that relationship rupture; he must respond.

Then king David answered and said, Call me Bathsheba. And she came into the king's presence, and stood before the king. And the king sware, and said, As the LORD liveth, that hath redeemed my soul out of all distress, Even as I sware unto thee by the LORD God of Israel, saying, Assuredly Solomon thy son shall reign after me, and he shall sit upon my throne in my stead; even so will I certainly do this day. Then Bathsheba bowed with her face to the earth, and did reverence to the king, and said, Let my lord king David live for ever. Guidance: David acts. Bathsheba had withdrawn during Nathan's audience; David summons her back. He swears an oath — the great Davidic oath form: 'As the LORD liveth, that hath redeemed my soul out of all distress.' He invokes the LORD as witness. Then the renewal of the previous oath: 'as I sware unto thee… Solomon thy son shall reign after me… even so will I certainly do this day.' The king's word is restored to its proper line; Solomon is the heir; today the matter will be settled. Bathsheba's response is the formal court answer of a queen-mother: 'Let my lord king David live for ever.' The Hebrew yechi adoni ha-melek David le-olam — the same yechi formula that the Adonijah party had been chanting for Adonijah; here she returns the formula to its rightful object. The chapter is showing us the king reawakening from his stupor. He has been a passive observer of his own kingdom for too long; the news of the coup has roused him; he is taking decisive action. The man who could barely stay warm under blankets is now issuing royal commands that will rewrite the succession in a single day.

The Hebrew yechi adoni ha-melek David le-olam — the same yechi formula that the Adonijah party had been chanting for Adonijah; here she returns the formula to its rightful object. The chapter is showing us the king reawakening from his stupor. He has been a passive observer of his own kingdom for too long; the news of the coup has roused him; he is taking decisive action. The man who could barely stay warm under blankets is now issuing royal commands that will rewrite the succession in a single day.

And king David said, Call me Zadok the priest, and Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada. And they came before the king. The king also said unto them, Take with you the servants of your lord, and cause Solomon my son to ride upon mine own mule, and bring him down to Gihon: And let Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet anoint him there king over Israel: and blow ye with the trumpet, and say, God save king Solomon. Then ye shall come up after him, that he may come and sit upon my throne; for he shall be king in my stead: and I have appointed him to be ruler over Israel and over Judah. And Benaiah the son of Jehoiada answered the king, and said, Amen: the LORD God of my lord the king say so too. As the LORD hath been with my lord the king, even so be he with Solomon, and make his throne greater than the throne of my lord the king David. Guidance: The coronation orders, issued with detailed precision. David sends for Zadok (priest), Nathan (prophet), and Benaiah (bodyguard commander) — the three institutional pillars Solomon needs. (1) Take David's own mule. Riding the king's personal mount is the public sign of royal designation. (2) Bring Solomon down to Gihon. Gihon is the spring on the east side of the city, the main water source of Jerusalem — and not the same spring as En-rogel where Adonijah is feasting. The geography matters: Adonijah's gathering is at En-rogel southeast; Solomon's anointing will be at Gihon east. The two events happen in earshot of each other. (3) Zadok and Nathan are to anoint Solomon king. (4) Blow the trumpet and proclaim 'God save king Solomon.' (5) Bring Solomon up to sit on David's throne. The protocol is complete and clear. Benaiah responds with one of the most beautiful courtier-blessings in the Bible: 'Amen: the LORD God of my lord the king say so too.' He echoes David's prayer with the LORD's own confirmation. Then his prayer for Solomon: 'As the LORD hath been with my lord the king, even so be he with Solomon, and make his throne greater than the throne of my lord the king David.' The captain of the bodyguard prays that the new king's reign will surpass the old. The chapter is recording the warmth of the loyalist faction; they are not merely going through political motions but praying earnestly for the LORD's blessing on the new king.

So Zadok the priest, and Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and the Cherethites, and the Pelethites, went down, and caused Solomon to ride upon king David's mule, and brought him to Gihon. And Zadok the priest took an horn of oil out of the tabernacle, and anointed Solomon. And they blew the trumpet; and all the people said, God save king Solomon. And all the people came up after him, and the people piped with pipes, and rejoiced with great joy, so that the earth rent with the sound of them. Guidance: The coronation procession to Gihon. Notice who goes: Zadok, Nathan, Benaiah, plus the Cherethites and Pelethites (David's foreign-mercenary bodyguard, fiercely loyal to him personally). Solomon rides David's mule — public proof of royal designation. At the Gihon spring, Zadok takes 'the horn of oil out of the tabernacle' — the consecrated anointing oil, kept in the tent of meeting. The detail is liturgically important; Solomon is being anointed with the priestly oil, sanctified for sacred use. The act locates Solomon's kingship inside the sacred order, not just the political order. He is not merely a king; he is the LORD's anointed (meshiach — messiah). The acclamation: 'God save king Solomon' — yechi ha-melek Shelomo. The trumpet blast. The people pipe with pipes (the chalil — flute-like instruments). 'The earth rent with the sound of them' — the noise was so great it shook the ground. The chapter is showing us a public, ecstatic coronation. Whatever Adonijah was attempting in private at En-rogel, Solomon's coronation is public, sanctioned, festive, and audible across the city.

And Adonijah and all the guests that were with him heard it as they had made an end of eating. And when Joab heard the sound of the trumpet, he said, Wherefore is this noise of the city being in an uproar? And while he yet spake, behold, Jonathan the son of Abiathar the priest came: and Adonijah said unto him, Come in; for thou art a valiant man, and bringest good tidings. And Jonathan answered and said to Adonijah, Verily our lord king David hath made Solomon king. And the king hath sent with him Zadok the priest, and Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and the Cherethites, and the Pelethites, and they have caused him to ride upon the king's mule: And Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king in Gihon: and they are come up from thence rejoicing, so that the city rang again. This is the noise that ye have heard. And also Solomon sitteth on the throne of the kingdom. And moreover the king's servants came to bless our lord king David, saying, God make the name of Solomon better than thy name, and make his throne greater than thy throne. And the king bowed himself upon the bed. And also thus said the king, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which hath given one to sit on my throne this day, mine eyes even seeing it. Guidance: The dramatic intercutting of scenes. Adonijah's feast is just finishing — coffee and dessert, so to speak — when the noise from the city center reaches them. Joab, the old commander, recognizes the sound for what it is: 'Wherefore is this noise of the city being in an uproar?' He hears the trumpet and the pipes and senses immediately that something organized has happened. Then Jonathan son of Abiathar arrives. Adonijah greets him with confident optimism: 'thou art a valiant man, and bringest good tidings.' (One of the most ironic 'famous last words' lines in the Old Testament — Adonijah assumes good news only because he refuses to imagine bad news.) Jonathan's report is detailed and devastating. (1) 'Our lord king David hath made Solomon king.' Royal authorization. (2) Lists the players: Zadok, Nathan, Benaiah, Cherethites, Pelethites. (3) 'They have caused him to ride upon the king's mule.' The most damning detail — David's own mule, the public proof of royal designation. (4) 'They have anointed him king in Gihon.' The priestly sanction. (5) 'Solomon sitteth on the throne of the kingdom.' He is already on the throne. (6) The court has come to bless David: 'God make the name of Solomon better than thy name.' (7) David's response: 'Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which hath given one to sit on my throne this day, mine eyes even seeing it.' The old king has had the joy of seeing his successor on his throne in his own lifetime. The chapter is showing us how thoroughly Adonijah's coup has been undone. There is nothing for him to do; the new king is already in place.

And Adonijah and all the guests that were with him heard it as they had made an end of eating. And when Joab heard the sound of the trumpet, he said, Wherefore is this noise of the city being in an uproar? And while he yet spake, behold, Jonathan the son of Abiathar the priest came: and Adonijah said unto him, Come in; for thou art a valiant man, and bringest good tidings. And Jonathan answered and said to Adonijah, Verily our lord king David hath made Solomon king. And the king hath sent with him Zadok the priest, and Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and the Cherethites, and the Pelethites, and they have caused him to ride upon the king's mule: And Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king in Gihon: and they are come up from thence rejoicing, so that the city rang again. This is the noise that ye have heard. And also Solomon sitteth on the throne of the kingdom. And moreover the king's servants came to bless our lord king David, saying, God make the name of Solomon better than thy name, and make his throne greater than thy throne. And the king bowed himself upon the bed. And also thus said the king, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which hath given one to sit on my throne this day, mine eyes even seeing it. And all the guests that were with Adonijah were afraid, and rose up, and went every man his way. And Adonijah feared because of Solomon, and arose, and went, and caught hold on the horns of the altar. And it was told Solomon, saying, Behold, Adonijah feareth king Solomon: for, lo, he hath caught hold on the horns of the altar, saying, Let king Solomon swear unto me today that he will not slay his servant with the sword. And Solomon said, If he will shew himself a worthy man, there shall not an hair of him fall to the earth: but if wickedness shall be found in him, he shall die. So king Solomon sent, and they brought him down from the altar. And he came and bowed himself to king Solomon: and Solomon said unto him, Go to thy house. Guidance: The collapse of Adonijah's coup. The guests scatter immediately — they were political followers; they have no commitment to Adonijah personally; with the LORD's anointed king now on the throne, they are exposed as conspirators. They flee. Adonijah himself flees to the altar — the great altar at the tent of meeting, with its four horns at the corners. The horns of the altar were a place of asylum (Exod 21:14 implies the custom; later confirmed by Joab's similar flight in 1 Kings 2:28). Grasping the horns meant the suppliant claimed sacred sanctuary; killing him at the altar would be sacrilege. Adonijah's request: 'Let king Solomon swear unto me today that he will not slay his servant with the sword.' He acknowledges Solomon as king (the title 'king Solomon' from his mouth is the formal submission); he asks for amnesty. Solomon's response is generous but conditional: 'If he will shew himself a worthy man (ben-chayil — son of strength, a man of character), there shall not an hair of him fall to the earth: but if wickedness shall be found in him, he shall die.' Solomon grants probationary mercy. Adonijah is brought from the altar, bows before Solomon (the political submission completed), and is sent to his own house. The chapter ends with both kings — outgoing David, incoming Solomon — alive and acknowledged; Adonijah alive and conditionally pardoned; the loyalist faction triumphant; the LORD's chosen succession secured. The very long chapter that started with David unable to keep warm ends with the kingdom safely in the hands of David's chosen son. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes are not stopped by old age or by usurpation; they work through the courage of Nathan, the boldness of Bathsheba, the loyalty of Benaiah, and the moral imagination of Solomon, who can grant mercy without giving up justice.
